segments.s407-g17: This is a sequence of nominal clauses with no copula expressed. 'تو شاهی' is a predicate-nominal construction meaning 'you are king' (copula omitted, typical in Persian). 'پدر من' is an appositive or vocative 'my father'. 'ترا بنده‌ام' is built from the 2nd-person bound pronoun + object marker (ت + را → ترا) plus بنده + ـام (first-person clitic), meaning 'I am a servant to you' or 'I am your servant.'
segments.s407-g18: 'همیشه' is an adverb ('always') at the clause start. 'به رای تو' is a prepositional phrase 'according to/at your counsel' (به + رای + possessive تو). 'پوینده‌ام' is a nominal predicate (پوینده 'seeker/follower') with the first-person suffix ـام, meaning 'I am a seeker/follower (of your counsel)'; the whole phrase = 'I am always a follower of your counsel.'
segments.s407-g19: 'تو دانی' is present second-person ('you know'); 'که' introduces the content clause ('that'). 'ارجاسپ' is the proper name 'Arjasp'. 'از بهر دین' is a circumstantial phrase 'for the sake of religion' (از بهر functions together as 'on account of/for').
segments.s407-g20: 'بیامد' is the simple past third-person singular 'he came'. 'چنان' ('so/thus') is a manner adverb modifying the coming. 'با سواران چین' is a prepositional phrase 'with the horsemen of China' (با + سواران + چین, where چین names the country/people), so the clause says he came with Chinese horsemen.
segments.s407-g21: 'بخوردم' is first-person past; literally 'I ate/consumed' but here used idiomatically to mean 'I took/underwent' or 'I endured' (a figurative use common in Persian poetry). It takes the object 'آن سخت سوگندها' = 'those severe oaths.'
segments.s407-g22: 'بپذرفتم' is first-person past 'I accepted'. It takes the object 'آن ایزدی پندها' = 'those divine counsels/admonitions' (ایزدی = 'divine', پندها = plural 'advices/counsels').
segments.s407-g23: The clause begins with 'که' introducing a generalizing subordinate clause. 'هرکس که' = 'whoever'. 'آرد' is third-person present/subjunctive 'brings/causes' (classical Persian uses this form in such clauses). 'به دین در شکست' is a circumstantial phrase 'to religion into defeat' (i.e. 'causes religion to be defeated' or 'brings about religion's downfall'). The whole phrase sets the condition: 'whoever brings religion to defeat...'.
segments.s407-g24: 'دلش' = 'his heart' (دل + possessive ـش). 'تاب گیرد' is a verbal phrase where تاب ('endurance/steadfastness') is said to be 'grasped/seized' by the heart; here it functions idiomatically as 'his heart is overcome/loses steadfastness' (گیرد is treated as passive 'be seized'). 'شود' is the auxiliary 'become', and 'بت‌پرست' is the resulting predicate noun 'an idolater'. Together: 'if his heart is overcome, he becomes an idolater.'
segments.s407-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning "when." بگذشت is the past action "passed" used of time (the night). شب is the subject "night." گرد کرده is a compound participial phrase (گرد here = "turned/going about") + کرده (past participle "having done"), so "having turned." عنان ("reins") is the object of گرد کرده, literal "having turned the reins." Together: "When the night had passed, (having) turned the reins..."
segments.s407-g2: برآورد is a literary perfective verb here meaning "brought forth/raised" or "drew forth." خورشید is the subject "the sun," and رخشان is an adjective "radiant." سنان is used metaphorically as "spear." The clause describes the sun producing or lifting its shining spear (a poetic image).
segments.s407-g3: نشست is the past verb "sat." The sequence از بر is an archaic/prepositional pairing used here to indicate position (lit. "from/on") and functions as "on/upon"; تخت زر is "gold (زَر) throne (تخت)" — "the golden throne." شهریار is the subject/title "the king." Word order is literary but means "the king sat on the golden throne."
segments.s407-g4: بشد is the past/occurrence sense of "شد" here rendering "there came/there stood." پیش او = "before him/in his presence" (پیش as "in the presence of"). فرخ is an epithet "fortunate/auspicious," and اسفندیار is the proper name. Together: "before him stood the fortunate Esfandiyar."
segments.s407-g5: همی بود is a literary/perfective progressive "was (still)" — همی adds continuity/emphasis. پیشش = "in his presence/before him." پرستارفش is an obscure/archaic lexical item (textual/lexical uncertainty is noted in the manuscript); grammatically it functions as a noun in apposition ("his attendant/worshipper" is a plausible gloss), placed after the clause.
segments.s407-g6: پراندیشه is an adjective meaning "full of thought/pensive." و is the coordinating "and." دست کرده is a participial construction "having put/with hand made (to...)" and به کش is a small prepositional phrase where کش (here) is used as an action-word meaning "(to) draw/pull" (i.e. draw a weapon). The whole clause: "pensive, and with his hand put to draw (his sword)."
segments.s407-g7: چو introduces a temporal clause "when." در پیش او = "in his presence/before him" (در + پیش). انجمن شد سپاه is a verbal phrase where انجمن (assembly/meeting) and شد (occurred/was made) govern سپاه (the army/host): "an assembly of troops was formed." The construction is literary inversion: "When an assembly of the army was formed before him..."
segments.s407-g8: ز is the poetic preposition "from/of." نام‌آوران is plural "the famed/renowned ones" (heroes). و is "and." ز گردان شاه = "from the king's regiments/troops" (گردان as "troop/regiment," شاه qualifying them). The phrase lists the composition of the assembly: "from the famed warriors and from the king's troops."
segments.s407-g81: مرا از بزرگان بر این شرم خاست is a compact classical-Persian clause meaning “shame arose for me before the great ones.” مرا is the preposed clitic pronoun + object marker expressing the experiencer/beneficiary ("for me" / "I felt"); بر این شرم is literally “upon this shame” (بر = upon, این = this, شرم = shame) and خاست is the archaic past of خاستن, here meaning “arose” or “began.” Word order (object/experiencer before predicate) and the archaic past verb are typical of Ferdowsi’s diction.
segments.s407-g82: که introduces a reported clause (“that …”). گویند is the archaic/poetic 3rd-person-plural verb meaning “they say” (used impersonally: ‘it is said’ / ‘they ask’). گنج و سپاهت کجاست is an indirect interrogative: گنج and سپاه with the possessive suffix -ت on سپاه mean “your treasure and your army,” کجا is “where,” and ست (the copula) attaches after the interrogative to form “where is ….” In sum: “that they say, ‘Where are your treasure and army?’.”
segments.s407-g83: بهانه کنون چیست is a nominal question “what excuse is there now” with the copula ست (here following the interrogative چی) forming چی + ست = چیست. من بر چیم follows: من (me/I) + بر (regarding/on) + چیم, an archaic fused interrogative/possessive meaning roughly “what (of) me” or “what claim do I have.” Together the phrase asks “What excuse do I now have in this matter?” Note the classical word order and the enclitic/interrogative form چیم.
segments.s407-g84: پس از رنج پویان is a temporal/causal phrase: پس از = “after,” رنج = “suffering/toil,” and پویان is a participial/poetic form meaning “of (my) quest / of seeking / of roaming” (i.e. the toils associated with seeking). ز بهر کیم = ز بهر (for the sake of) + کیم (archaic interrogative “whom/for whom”), so the clause asks “after the toil of my quest, for whom (am I doing this)?” The line uses poetic/archaic prepositions (ز بهر) and an older interrogative form (کیم).
segments.s407-g57: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'. The phrase 'ز ترکان گریزان شده' is a prefixed participial clause (گریزان + شده) describing شهریار; شـده is the periphrastic/auxiliary 'become/has become', so the whole means 'having become fleeing from the Turks' → 'fleeing from the Turks', placed before the noun it modifies (شهریار).
segments.s407-g58: همی is a literary marker of continuous or habitual action ('keeps/continually'); پیچد is the older-present form of پیچیدن (3rd sg.), 'he twists/turns' (here 'twists about/struggles'). از introduces separation/motion away ('out of/from') and بند اسفندیار is a genitive construction: 'the fetter/bond of Esfandiyar'.
segments.s407-g59: نسوزد is a negative jussive/optative form meaning 'may it not burn' or 'let it not ache' and here expresses a wish/admonition; دلت is دل + second-person suffix -ت 'your heart'; بر is the preposition 'over/at/concerning'; چنین کارها = 'such deeds/affairs'. Note poetic verb-before-subject order.
segments.s407-g60: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase (به + این) meaning 'with these' or 'by these'; it governs the following list. درد و تیمار و آزارها is a triad of nouns joined by و: 'pain(s), care/solicitude, and annoyances/torments', giving the nature of the suffering referred to.
segments.s407-g61: سخنها is the plural 'words/speeches'. جز این literally 'except/aside from this' qualifies what follows. نیز adds 'also/in addition'. بسیار modifies the verb and means 'much/many'. گفت is the simple past third-person 'he said' (poetic past: he spoke).
segments.s407-g62: که here is the conjunction 'for/that' introducing an explanatory clause. گفتار 'speech' + با 'with' + درد و غم 'pain and sorrow' is the complement; بود is the past copula 'was'. جفت is used adjectivally/nominally to mean 'paired/paired with' (i.e. his speech was matched with sorrow).
segments.s407-g63: غل و بند lists two synonyms 'fetters and bonds'. بر هم (here two separate words) means 'together/one upon another' (i.e. all at once). شکستم is 1st-person past 'I broke', and همه after the verb emphasizes 'all of them / entirely'.
segments.s407-g64: دوان آمدم is a verbal-adverbial combination meaning 'I ran/rushed' (دوان = running/at speed, آمدم = I came). نزد + شاه means 'in the presence of the king/near the king'; رمه appended indicates the throng/camp/host—that is, he ran to the king and his throng.
segments.s407-g25: میانش is میان + enclitic -ش (‘his/it’), literally ‘his middle’; the phrase places the target point first. به خنجر uses به as an instrumental 'by/with' (here introducing the weapon). کنم is the present-subjunctive/optative form of کردن used to state the speaker's resolve or intent ('I will/do'), and به دو نیم means 'into two halves' with به = 'into/towards'. Word order is somewhat flexible and poetic: object/target (میانش) + instrument (به خنجر) + verb (کنم) + result (به دو نیم).
segments.s407-g26: نباشد is the negative of باشد used as an existential/optative ('may there not be' / 'there is not'); here it functions as a declarative negative existence: 'there will not be'. مرا (split as م + را in the listing) is the object/beneficiary 'me' (مرا = 'for me' / 'to me'), with را as the definite/object marker that follows the pronoun. از کسی = 'from/of anyone' marks the source/agent of fear, and ترس و بیم are two near-synonyms standing in apposition ('fear and dread'). The literal order is 'there-not (is) for-me from anyone fear and dread' → 'I shall have no fear of anyone.'
segments.s407-g27: وزان پس is an archaic / poetic connective ('and from that / thereafter'); که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that'. ارجاسپ is the proper name (subject). آمد is simple past 'came'. به جنگ uses به as movement/toward 'to (the) battle'. The clause means 'and after that, when Arjasp came to battle'.
segments.s407-g28: نبرگشتم is برگشتم 'I returned/turned back' with the negative prefix ن-, so it means 'I did not turn back / I did not retreat.' از جنگ = 'from the battle/fight.' دشتی پلنگ is a descriptive compound 'plains leopard' (دشتی modifying پلنگ); here it functions as a vocative or epithet. The whole segment: 'I did not turn back from the fight, O leopard of the plain.'
segments.s407-g29: مرا (split in the listing) is the accusative 'me' (مرا). خوار کردی is the perfect/past 'you made (me) low/despised' ('you humiliated me'). به + گفت = 'by the saying / with the words' (به used instrumentally/causal), and گرزم is گرز + -م which here functions as 'my mace' (or the notion 'I will strike with my mace'); in context it is the content of the saying. Thus: 'You humiliated me by saying “(I will) with my mace...”'.
segments.s407-g30: که introduces the explanatory clause 'because/that.' جام خورش is 'the cup of his stew/food' (خورش with the possessive -ش = 'his cooked dish'); جام = 'cup.' خواستی is past 'you wanted.' روز بزم = 'the day of the feast/banquet' (روز 'day' + بزم 'feast'). Together: 'because you wanted his cup of stew on the day of the feast.'
segments.s407-g31: ببستی is a past/pf. form meaning 'you bound/tied' (the listing gives both 'closed' and 'bound'; here it is 'bound'). تن من = 'my body' (تن 'body' + من 'my'). به بند گران uses به instrumentally 'with/in' and بند گران = 'heavy fetters' (بند = fetter, گران = heavy/weighty). The verb is transitive with the following phrase as the instrument/location to which the body was bound.
segments.s407-g32: ستونها = 'the pillars/columns' (plural). و = 'and.' مسمار = 'nail/peg' (here likely large iron nails). آهنگران = 'the blacksmiths / ironsmiths' (agents who made/installed the nails). Grammatically this phrase names the things (pillars and iron nails) to which the speaker's body was fastened.
segments.s407-g65: از ایشان (literally “of/from them”) marks the group affected; بکشتم is a past first‑person verb (classical/poetic past “I slew/struck” — here transitive). فزون از شمار is an adverbial comparative phrase “more than the number,” modifying بکشتم; the phrase order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (prepositional phrase + verb + comparative).
segments.s407-g66: ز کردار من is a causal prepositional phrase “because of my deeds” (ز = because/of); شاد شد is a past-tense/perfective resultative ‘became glad’ and شهریار is the logical subject, placed after the verb (verse word order). من follows کردار to mark possession (‘my deeds’).
segments.s407-g67: گر is the conditional marker “if.” از هفتخوان gives the domain “of/from the Seven Trials”; برشمارم is a first‑person verb ‘I count/recount’ in a conditional/optative sense (‘were I to enumerate’ or ‘if I enumerate’). سخن is the object ‘tale/story/speech.’ The verb appears before the noun for metrical/poetic ordering.
segments.s407-g68: همانا که is an emphatic introducer (“verily/indeed that”); هرگز ... نیاید is a negated future/irrealis sense ‘would never come’; به بن is an idiom meaning ‘to the base/end’ (i.e. reach a conclusion). Together: “indeed that it would never reach an end.”
segments.s407-g69: ز تن (literally “from the body”) marks source/partitive. باز کردم here functions as a separative/transitive verbal phrase ‘I opened/removed/detached’ (poetic usage of باز + کردن), with سر ارجاسپ را as the definite object (را marks it). Word order puts the source phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s407-g70: برافراختم is a past first‑person verb ‘I lifted/exalted/displayed.’ نام گشتاسپ را is the definite object (را) but rendered as ‘the name/fame of Gushtasp’; نام here has the nuance of reputation/fame rather than merely the label. The verb precedes its object as common in poetic diction.
segments.s407-g71: زن و کودکانش is a coordinated noun phrase ‘wife and his children’ (کودکانش = ‘his children’). بدین is the prepositional phrase ‘to this’ (poetic contraction of به + این) and بارگاه is the destination ‘court/palace’; together: ‘to this court.’ The preposition+demonstrative unit is fused for metrical reasons in the poem.
segments.s407-g72: بیاوردم is past first‑person ‘I brought/led (in).’ آن stresses ‘that/the’ (demonstrative). The list گنج و تخت و کلاه is a simple coordinated direct object: ‘treasure and throne and helmet,’ with normal connective و; the verb preceding the object(s) is typical of verse order.
segments.s407-g73: همه نیکویها بکردی به گنج is a clause in which the object phrase 'همه نیکویها' (literally 'all the good things/virtues') precedes the verb 'بکردی' (second-person past: 'you did / you made'). The preposition 'به' here has the sense of motion/result ('into/towards') and links the verb to its complement 'گنج' (treasure): 'you made all goodness into treasure.'
segments.s407-g74: مرا مایه خون آمد و درد و رنج: 'مرا' is the affected person ('to me/for me') marked by the object marker 'را' (here split as 'م' + 'را' in the segmentation). The verb 'آمد' (came/happened) introduces a result: 'it came to me' meaning 'it brought me' or 'it became for me'. 'مایه' = 'cause/source', so 'مایه خون آمد' = 'it became a cause of bloodshed'; the coordinating 'و' links the further nouns 'درد و رنج' (pain and suffering).
segments.s407-g75: ز بس بند و سوگند و پیمان تو: 'ز' = 'because of/from' introduces the reason. 'بس' here is an intensifier ('so much/of so many'), and it modifies the list 'بند و سوگند و پیمان' ('bonds, oaths and pledges'). The final 'تو' marks possession/agent: 'your bonds, oaths, and pledges.'
segments.s407-g76: همی نگذرم من ز فرمان تو: 'همی' is a frequentative/intensifying adverb ('still/indeed/continually'). The verb 'نگذرم' (negated present/subjunctive form) here means 'I will not forgo / I will not let pass / I will not abandon' (i.e., 'I will not forgive/abandon'). 'من' is the subject 'I', and 'ز فرمان تو' ('from/of your command') is the thing not to be abandoned. Word order places the verb before the subject for emphasis, typical of poetic diction.
segments.s407-g77: همی گفتی ار باز بینم ترا: 'همی گفتی' is a habitual or repeated past ('you used to say' / 'you repeatedly said'). 'ار' = 'if', and 'باز' = 'again' (here 'once more'). 'بینم' is a first‑person present/subjunctive form meaning 'I see' (used here as the protasis of the conditional). 'ترا' (segmented as suffix + 'را') is the object 'you'. The clause is the protasis: 'If I see you again...'.
segments.s407-g78: ز روشن‌روان برگزینم ترا: 'ز' = 'from/out of', 'روشن‌روان' is a compound adjective/noun 'bright‑souled' (i.e., fair-minded or noble), and 'برگزینم' is the first‑person present/subjunctive ('I will choose / I shall select'). Together this is the apodosis: 'I will choose you from among the bright‑souled.' The object 'ترا' is again marked by the suffix + 'را'.
segments.s407-g79: سپارم ترا افسر و تخت عاج: 'سپارم' is first‑person present/subjunctive ('I will entrust / I shall deliver'), followed by the object 'ترا' ('to you') and then the things entrusted 'افسر و تخت عاج' ('the helmet and the ivory throne'). Word order places the verb before its indirect object and then the direct objects, a common poetic order.
segments.s407-g80: که هستی به مردی سزاوار تاج: 'که' introduces a clause of explanation ('for / since / because'), 'هستی' here functions as the copular/being notion ('you are' / 'being'), 'به مردی' indicates the standard or quality ('by/with manhood' i.e. 'by your manliness'), and 'سزاوار تاج' means 'worthy of the crown.' The clause explains the reason the speaker will entrust the regalia.
segments.s407-g33: سوی (prepositional) + گنبدان (place) + دژ (noun) form a prepositional phrase 'toward/to the fortress of Gonbadān'; فرستادیم is past first‑person plural 'we sent/dispatched'. The verb follows the place phrase; the direct object is not repeated here but carried over into the next clause.
segments.s407-g34: ز (poetic 'from' / marking cause or manner) + خواری 'humiliation' gives the manner ('in humiliation'); به marks the recipient, بدکارگان 'the wrongdoers'; دادیم is past 1pl 'we gave/handed over'. Together: 'and we, in humiliation, handed (him) over to the evildoers.'
segments.s407-g35: به زاول ('به' + زاول) = 'from the outset / at first'; شدی is second‑person past 'you became / you were' used to place the subject in that initial time; بلخ بگذاشتی = 'you put aside / abandoned Balkh' where بگذاشتی is past 2nd‑singular. Overall: 'You, from the first, abandoned Balkh.'
segments.s407-g36: همه رزم را = 'the whole/entire war' with را marking definiteness; بزم is a predicate noun 'a banquet/feast'; پنداشتی is past 2nd‑singular 'you thought/considered'. Word order places the object before the predicate and final verb: 'you considered the whole battle a feast.'
segments.s407-g37: بدیدی is past 2nd‑singular 'you saw'; همی is an emphatic adverb ('indeed/just' here); the object تیغِ ارجاسپ را 'Arjasp's sword' follows (ارجاسپ marked by را). The clause reads: 'You indeed saw Arjasp's sword.'
segments.s407-g38: فگندی is an archaic/dialectal past form (here functioning as 'you stained/you cast/you hurled'); به خون = 'with/into blood' indicating result/manner; پیر لهراسپ را 'the old Lohrasp' is the definite object (را). Together: 'you drenched / stained old Lohrasp with blood.'
segments.s407-g39: چو = temporal 'when'; جاماسپ آمد = 'Jamasp came' (past); مرا بسته دید = 'he saw me bound' where بسته is a participle/adjective 'bound/tied' and دید is past 'saw'. The whole clause: 'When Jamasp came, he saw me bound.'
segments.s407-g40: وزان = 'and from that / from those (things)' linking to the preceding sight; بستگیها 'the bindings/fastenings' (plural) is the source; تنم 'my body' (patient) + خسته 'weary/exhausted' is the state observed; دید 'he saw' closes the clause: 'and from those bindings he saw my body exhausted.'
segments.s407-g41: مرا پادشاهی پذیرفت is an SOV clause with the object pronoun ('مرا') placed before the verb for emphasis: 'مرا' = 'me' (accusative), 'پادشاهی' = 'kingship/being king' as the complement, and 'پذیرفت' is 3rd-person singular past 'he accepted/took (as)'. The final 'تخت' begins the next clause and names the thing (the throne) that the following phrase will qualify.
segments.s407-g42: بران (classical contraction of بر آن) = 'for/on/with respect to that (throne)' and picks up تخت from the previous segment; 'نیز' = 'also'; 'چندی' here is an adverbial phrase 'for a while'; 'بکوشید' is past tense (he strove/attempted), where the prefixed ب- is a classical formation found in epic/style; 'سخت' is adverbial here = 'hard/strongly'.
segments.s407-g43: بدو = 'to him' (dative). گفتم is the speaker's verb 'I said' (first-person past) even though the provided lemma senses list gives nouns; syntactically it governs the reported noun phrase that follows. این بندهای گران = 'these heavy bonds/fetters' (این 'these' + بندهای plural + گران 'heavy').
segments.s407-g44: به here functions instrumentally/associative = 'with' or 'by means of'; زنجیر و مسمار = 'chains and nails' (two instruments joined by و); آهنگران is a post-nominal genitive/agent meaning 'of the blacksmiths' (nails made by blacksmiths). The whole phrase gives the means for fastening the bonds.
segments.s407-g45: بمانم is first-person present-subjunctive/optative used in classical Persian to express 'I will remain' or 'let me remain' — here a resolute future-like sense. چنین هم = 'thus, indeed'; به فرمان شاه = prepositional phrase 'by/on the king's command' (به used as 'by/under').
segments.s407-g46: نخواهم is the negative first-person form ('I will not wish/accept'), repeated for parallelism before سپاه and again before کلاه; this expresses refusal rather than mere lack of desire. سپاه = 'army/troops'; کلاه here denotes the helmet (symbolic of military command) rather than a trick.
segments.s407-g47: به یزدان نمایم = 'I will show/present (it) to God': به is the dative 'to', یزدان = 'God', and نمایم (first-person present/subjunctive) functions as a future/intentional 'I will show'. به‌روز شمار is an adverbial compound meaning 'day-by-day' or 'reckon the days' (به‌روز 'of the day' + شمار 'count'), describing how the speaker will make the matter known or keep account.
segments.s407-g48: بنالم is first-person present-subjunctive/optative of نالیدن 'to lament/complain' used here as 'I will lament/complain'. ز marks cause or object 'because of/about'; بدگوی = 'the slanderer' (the person who speaks ill); با کردگار — in classical usage با can function as a dative/locution meaning 'to' or 'before' in a religious/legal sense, so با کردگار = 'to the Creator' (i.e., I will complain to God about the slanderer).
segments.s407-g49: مرا را here functions together as the indirect object 'to me' (the data split 'م'+'را' — in normal Persian 'مرا'). گفت‌گر is a poetic sequence: گفت (said) + گر (if) introducing a quoted conditional (‘he said that if…’). پند من is a noun phrase 'my advice' with من as a possessor, and نشنوی is the 2nd‑person present negative 'you do not hear / you do not heed', completing the conditional clause introduced by گر.
segments.s407-g50: بسازی is a 2nd‑person present/future form 'you will make / you will build' (verb without an overt pronoun). ابر تخت is a compound noun phrase 'throne of clouds' (modifier precedes or forms a unit in poetry). بر بدخوی uses the preposition بر 'upon/for' plus بدخوی 'the ill‑tempered' — the whole clause is the result clause of the earlier conditional: 'you will build [a] cloud‑throne upon the ill‑tempered.'
segments.s407-g51: دگر گفت functions as a reporting formula 'another said' or 'then another said'. کز is the archaic subordinating particle 'that/which' introducing the content clause. خون چندان means 'so much blood' (خون in the sense of blood/bloodshed and چنان 'so/much'), and سران is plural 'chiefs / heads' (the subject of the following descriptive phrase). Note word order: the subordinate clause is fronted immediately after the reporting phrase.
segments.s407-g52: سرافراز is an adjectival epithet 'proud / lofty / honored' qualifying سران from the previous segment. با is the preposition 'with,' marking the instrument or accompaniment, گرزهای is the plural 'maces/clubs' and گران is the attributive adjective 'heavy' (post‑nominal), so the phrase reads 'proud chiefs, with heavy maces.' The whole phrase completes the description started in the previous clause.
segments.s407-g53: بران is a poetic form meaning 'on that / upon that' (equivalent to بر آن), introducing location. رزمگه is 'battlefield'; خسته is a past‑participle/adjective 'weary/exhausted' describing the person(s) in that place. تنها 'alone/only' modifies the phrase, and به تیر uses به with تیر as an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/with an arrow' ('تنها به تیر' = 'by the arrow alone' or 'only by an arrow'). Together this gives the circumstance in which the action occurred.
segments.s407-g54: همان is an emphatic demonstrative 'that very / the same'; خواهرانت is 'your sisters' (خواهران + possessive/2nd‑person suffix), and ببرده is a perfective/past form meaning 'has carried off / has taken away' (here rendered as 'took'). اسیر is a predicative/adjectival noun 'captive.' The clause therefore states the result: 'he took those very sisters of yours captive.'
segments.s407-g55: دگر here is an adverb 'again' / 'another time.' گرد in this context heads the nominal phrase (poetic use; English gloss 'the round/host' or simply part of a compound epithet) and آزاده is an epithet 'noble / free‑born' modifying the name فرشیدورد (a personal name). The whole segment introduces the agent: 'again the noble Farshidvord…'.
segments.s407-g56: فگندست is a past transitive verb 'threw' (classical/epic past). خسته is the object/secondary description 'the exhausted / the weary ones' and به دشت نبرد is a prepositional phrase 'into the plain of battle' (به = into/toward, دشت 'plain', نبرد 'battle' qualifying دشت as 'battle‑plain'). The clause describes the action Farshidvord performed.
segments.s407-g9: همه موبدان پیش او بر رده is a noun phrase that sets up the participants and their disposition. همه + موبدان is the plural subject ('all the mōbeds'); پیش او is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in his presence / before him' placed after the noun (poetic word order); بر رده ('on/into rows') is a locative phrase describing how they stood. There is no finite verb here — the predication is completed by the following segment (elliptical verb elsewhere).
segments.s407-g10: This clause supplies the predication: ز اسپهبدان پیش او صف زده. ز + اسپهبدان 'by/from the spahbeds' names the group, پیش او repeats the locative 'before him', and صف زده is a past-participial/perfective phrase ('lined up', literally 'line-struck') functioning as the predicate. The main finite verb is omitted (elliptical); صف زده works as a stative/resultant description ('had formed ranks').
segments.s407-g11: پس is a temporal/conjunctive adverb 'then'; اسفندیار is the subject (proper name); آن یل پیلتن is an appositive/epithet 'that hero of Piltan' where آن is the demonstrative modifying یل (hero) and پیلتن is a place-name or epithet. Word order places the adverb and subject before the epithet in typical epic style.
segments.s407-g12: برآورد here is a past/completed action meaning 'raised/uttered' (from the verb برآوردن) and serves as the main verb 'he uttered / he raised (words)'. از درد is a causal/prepositional phrase 'because of / from pain' indicating motive. آنگه is an adverb 'then/thereupon' and سخن is the object 'speech / words' — the phrase order (verb + از-phrase + adverb + object) is poetic but grammatical: 'he uttered, out of pain, then words.'
segments.s407-g13: بدو گفت is a two-part sequence: بدو = 'to him / unto him' (dative/unindependent pronoun) and گفت = 'he said' (past tense). شاها is the vocative 'O king!'. انوشه بدی is compressed/poetic: انوشه can function as an adjective or name meaning 'everlasting / Anoushe' and بدی here is an archaic/personal-form or verbal form; in context the phrase reads as a predicative or immediate object meaning roughly 'you have bestowed/are the source of something everlasting.' This segment is somewhat compressed poetically — verbs or copulas may be elliptical or archaic.
segments.s407-g14: توی بر زمین فره ایزدی is a locative nominal clause: توی ('in / upon') + بر زمین ('on the earth') gives the spatial frame, and فره ایزدی ('the divine farr' — فره = divine glory, ایزدی = divine) is the theme/topic. There is no separate verb in this segment; it completes the preceding speech (i.e., '... on the earth the divine farr [is manifest / you have caused]').
segments.s407-g15: سر داد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: سر (literally 'head' but here in idiom 'a sound/utterance') + داد ('gave') together mean 'he uttered / raised his voice'. و links clauses. مهر از تو 'love / favour because of you' is the theme; پیدا شدست is an old/poetic perfect/resultative 'has become visible / has appeared' (شدست = 'it became'). The tense/aspect is perfect/resultative describing the present manifest state.
segments.s407-g16: همان is an emphatic/demonstrative 'the very / that same'. تاج و تخت is a coordinated NP 'crown and throne'. از تو ('because of you' / 'from you') gives the cause, and زیبا شدست is the predicate 'have become beautiful' with شدست as the archaic/resultative copula 'has become'. The verb targets the inanimate theme (crown and throne) and expresses resulting change.

sentences.s407-s9: You are king, my father; I am your servant; I am always a follower of your counsel.
sentences.s407-s10: You know that Arjasp, for the sake of religion, came so with the horsemen of China.
sentences.s407-s11: I endured those harsh oaths; I accepted those divine counsels.
sentences.s407-s12: That whoever brings religion to defeat — if his heart is overcome — becomes an idolater.
sentences.s407-s1: When the night had passed, having turned the reins, the radiant sun drew forth its shining spear.
sentences.s407-s2: The king sat upon the golden throne; before him stood the fortunate Esfandiyar.
sentences.s407-s3: He was still before him — his attendant (or worshipper) — pensive, with his hand laid to draw (his weapon).
sentences.s407-s4: When an assembly of the troops came together before him, from the renowned warriors and from the king's regiments.
sentences.s407-s41: I was ashamed before the nobles at this: they say, "Where are your treasure and your army?"
sentences.s407-s42: What excuse do I have now in this? After the hardships of my quest, for whom am I (struggling)?
sentences.s407-s29: The king, fleeing from the Turks, kept twisting to free himself from Esfandiyar's fetter.
sentences.s407-s30: May your heart not burn over such deeds, with these pains, cares, and torments.
sentences.s407-s31: He spoke many other words besides this, for his speech was paired with pain and sorrow.
sentences.s407-s32: I broke all fetters and bonds; I ran and came to the king's throng.
sentences.s407-s13: I will cut him in two with my dagger; I shall have no fear or dread of anyone.
sentences.s407-s14: And after that, when Arjasp came to battle, I did not turn back from the fight, O leopard of the plain.
sentences.s407-s15: You humiliated me with the words 'I'll strike with my mace,' because you wanted his cup of stew on the day of the feast.
sentences.s407-s16: You bound my body in heavy fetters to the pillars and the nails of the blacksmiths.
sentences.s407-s33: I slew more of them than could be counted; the king was glad because of my deeds.
sentences.s407-s34: If I were to recount the story of the Seven Trials—truly it would never come to an end.
sentences.s407-s35: I tore Arjasp’s head from his body; I exalted the name of Gushtasp.
sentences.s407-s36: I brought his wife and children to this court, and I brought that treasure, the throne, and the helmet.
sentences.s407-s37: You turned all good things into treasure; for me it brought bloodshed, pain, and suffering.
sentences.s407-s38: Because of your many bonds, oaths, and pledges, I will not abandon (or forgive) your command.
sentences.s407-s39: You used to say, 'If I see you again, I will choose you from the bright‑souled.'
sentences.s407-s40: I will entrust to you the helmet and the ivory throne, for by your manhood you are worthy of the crown.
sentences.s407-s17: We sent him to the fortress of Gonbadān and, in humiliation, handed him over to the wrongdoers.
sentences.s407-s18: From the very outset you forsook Balkh; you took the whole war for a revel.
sentences.s407-s19: You indeed saw Arjasp's sword, and you stained the old Lohrasp with blood.
sentences.s407-s20: When Jamasp came, he saw me bound; and from those bindings he saw my body exhausted.
sentences.s407-s21: He accepted me as king, and he also strove hard for that throne for a while.
sentences.s407-s22: I told him, 'Fasten these heavy bonds with chains and the blacksmiths' nails.'
sentences.s407-s23: I will remain thus; even by the king's command I will not accept troops nor a helmet.
sentences.s407-s24: I will lay the matter before God and reckon the days; I will lament to the Creator about the slanderer.
sentences.s407-s25: He said to me, 'If you do not heed my counsel, you will build a throne of clouds for the ill‑tempered.'
sentences.s407-s26: Another said that from so much blood the proud chiefs, with heavy maces...
sentences.s407-s27: On that battlefield, weary and alone, by an arrow he took those very sisters of yours captive.
sentences.s407-s28: Again the noble Farshidvord hurled the exhausted into the plain of battle.
sentences.s407-s5: All the mobeds stood in order before him; the spahbeds likewise had formed ranks before him.
sentences.s407-s6: Then Esfandiyar, that hero of Piltan, because of the pain raised his voice and spoke.
sentences.s407-s7: He said to him, 'O king — you have bestowed the everlasting (gift); on the earth is the divine farr.'
sentences.s407-s8: He raised his voice, and love (affection/favour) has appeared because of you; indeed the crown and throne have become fair because of you.
